statement.markdown: Given an integer array `nums`, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence. A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from another sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. For example, `[3,6,9]` is a subsequence of `[1,2,3,6,7,9]`.

hints.text: The problem asks for the longest strictly increasing subsequence, which implies that we need to compare elements and keep track of the longest sequence found so far.
A dynamic programming approach could be suitable here, as the problem has overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure. Think about how you can break down the problem into smaller subproblems.
Consider using a DP table where each entry dp[i] represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i. Think about how you can update dp[i] based on previous entries.

editorial.title: The Longest Increasing Subsequence Problem: A Study of Brute Force and Optimal Solutions
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the longest subsequence in an array of integers where each subsequent element is greater than the previous one. A brute force approach would involve checking all possible subsequences, but this is highly inefficient. A key observation is that the problem can be solved using dynamic programming, where the solution to a subproblem depends on the solutions to smaller subproblems.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves generating all possible subsequences and checking if they are increasing. This approach is correct but has an exponential time complexity due to the large number of subsequences to check. Specifically, for an array of length n, there are 2^n possible subsequences, making this approach impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses dynamic programming to store the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at each position in the array. This allows us to avoid recomputing the same subproblems multiple times, significantly reducing the time complexity.
editorial.optimalSolution: The dynamic programming approach works by initializing an array dp where dp[i] represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i. We then iterate through the array, updating dp[i] based on the values of dp[j] for j < i where nums[i] > nums[j]. The maximum value in dp represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming approach can be proven by induction. The base case is when the array has only one element, in which case the longest increasing subsequence has length 1. For the inductive step, assume that the approach works for arrays of length k. For an array of length k+1, the approach still works because it considers all previous elements and updates dp[k+1] accordingly. Thus, the approach is correct for arrays of all lengths.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the dynamic programming approach with sample inputs, such as [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18], yields the correct result of 4, corresponding to the subsequence [2, 3, 7, 101].
commonMistakes: Not initializing the dp array correctly, leading to incorrect results.
Failing to consider all previous elements when updating dp[i].
Not returning the maximum value in dp as the result.
Incorrectly handling edge cases, such as an empty input array.
Using a different dynamic programming state that does not lead to an efficient solution.
